lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who’s there, besides foul weather?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: One minded like the weather, most unquietly.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I know you. Where’s the king?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Contending with the fretful element:

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Bids the winds blow the earth into the sea,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Or swell the curled water ’bove the main,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: That things might change or cease; tears his white hair,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Which the impetuous blasts, with eyeless rage,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Catch in their fury, and make nothing of;

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Strives in his little world of man to out-scorn

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The to-and-fro-conflicting wind and rain.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: This night, wherein the cub-drawn bear would couch,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The lion and the belly-pinched wolf

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Keep their fur dry, unbonneted he runs,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And bids what will take all.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: But who is with him?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: None but the fool; who labours to out-jest

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: His heart-struck injuries.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Sir, I do know you;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And dare, upon the warrant of my note,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Commend a dear thing to you. There is division,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Although as yet the face of it be cover’d

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: With mutual cunning, ’twixt Albany and Cornwall;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who have—as who have not, that their great stars

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Throned and set high?—servants, who seem no less,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Which are to France the spies and speculations

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Intelligent of our state; what hath been seen,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Either in snuffs and packings of the dukes,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Or the hard rein which both of them have borne

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Against the old kind king; or something deeper,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Whereof perchance these are but furnishings;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: But, true it is, from France there comes a power

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Into this scatter’d kingdom; who already,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Wise in our negligence, have secret feet

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: In some of our best ports, and are at point

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To show their open banner. Now to you:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: If on my credit you dare build so far

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To make your speed to Dover, you shall find

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Some that will thank you, making just report

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Of how unnatural and bemadding sorrow

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The king hath cause to plain.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I am a gentleman of blood and breeding;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And, from some knowledge and assurance, offer

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: This office to you.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: I will talk further with you.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: No, do not.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: For confirmation that I am much more

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Than my out-wall, open this purse, and take

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: What it contains. If you shall see Cordelia—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: As fear not but you shall—show her this ring;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And she will tell you who your fellow is

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That yet you do not know. Fie on this storm!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I will go seek the king.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Give me your hand: have you no more to say?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Few words, but, to effect, more than all yet;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That, when we have found the king—in which your pain

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That way, I’ll this—he that first lights on him

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Holla the other.

stage_directions.text: Storm still. Enter Kent and a Gentleman, meeting.

glosses.definition: blind fury — the wind as a blind beast
a bear whose teats have been drained dry by cubs
Lear ripping his own hair in the storm
the storm battering from all sides

annotations.title: France invades — the political machinery that will fail the play
annotations.body: Kent's speech to the Gentleman announces the French invasion — the force that might redeem Lear politically if it arrives in time. Shakespeare wants the audience to register the military stakes without dwelling on them; the invasion will matter as a deadline, not as a plot-driver. By 5.3 the French will have lost, Cordelia will be captured, and the political frame will collapse back onto private grief. This scene is a setup whose payoff is its own futility: the armies matter less than whether Lear's button gets undone.
sources: G. K. Hunter, 'The Last Tragic Heroes' in Later Shakespeare (1966)

annotations.title: The Gentleman's report of Lear on the heath — ekphrasis of the storm
annotations.body: The Gentleman's description of Lear in the storm — 'Contending with the fretful elements; / Bids the wind blow the earth into the sea' — is Shakespeare's preparation for what we are about to see directly. This is the classical technique of ekphrasis: a verbal painting that primes the spectator for the scene that follows. The Gentleman gives us the storm's geography ('this night, wherein the cub-drawn bear would couch'), the weather's refusal ('the lion, and the belly-pinched wolf, / Keep their fur dry'), and Lear's specific posture (tearing his hair, running bareheaded). When the storm scenes of 3.2 and 3.4 arrive, the audience already has the image; the poetry can now do intensification rather than description. Shakespeare uses ekphrasis deliberately before his greatest staged set-pieces.
sources: Russ McDonald, Shakespeare and the Arts of Language (2001)

trials.prompt: What political news does Kent deliver in 3.1?
options: France has landed an invading army — the political machinery that might redeem Lear, though the play will let it fail

trials.prompt: The Gentleman's description of Lear on the heath in 3.1 uses the classical technique of —
options: Ekphrasis — verbal painting that primes the audience for the storm scene to follow, so that the staged storm can intensify rather than describe
